title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption:
History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita, 1000 to 2018

Unit 1 ‘The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]

Across more than a millennium, real gross domestic product per capita — measured in 2011 US dollars adjusted for purchasing power parity — remained nearly flat for most of the world until the last two centuries, then surged upward in select economies. From year 1000 through roughly 1750, all visible lines hover near the bottom of the vertical axis, clustered between $0 and $3,000, with minor fluctuations. Italy’s green line shows slight variation between 1300 and 1600, dipping and rising within that narrow band. After 1800, the United Kingdom’s blue line begins a steady climb, crossing $5,000 by 1850 and accelerating past $10,000 after 1900. Japan’s orange line remains low until 1950, then rises steeply, overtaking the UK around 1990 and reaching just under $40,000 by 2018. China’s purple line stays near zero until 1980, then climbs rapidly to about $15,000 by 2018. India’s black line follows a similar late surge, ending near $7,000. The vertical scale spans from $0 to $40,000, with gridlines marking every $5,000 increment. All five countries show minimal growth for the first 800 years shown, then diverge sharply after 1800, with Japan and the UK reaching the highest levels by 2018, while China and India remain substantially lower despite recent acceleration. The dominant pattern is prolonged stagnation followed by rapid, uneven growth concentrated in the last 200 years.
